summary: Gruber explains why copy and paste is his favorite feature in iPhone OS 3.0, detailing how he uses his iPhone to post links and entries to Daring Fireball via Movable Type's mobile web interface. Before copy and paste, adding URLs, blockquotes, or additional links to posts from the iPhone was painstaking or impossible. He praises Apple's implementation — text selection via the magnifier loupe and contextual pop-up commands — as elegant solutions to genuinely hard interaction design problems. He argues that Apple's two-year delay exemplifies their philosophy of shipping nothing rather than shipping something mediocre, contrasting this with Microsoft's approach.
key_points: Copy and paste transformed Gruber's mobile blogging workflow, making it practical to add URLs, blockquotes, and additional links from the iPhone
The interaction design challenges — selecting text and invoking commands without a keyboard or menu bar — were genuinely difficult, and Apple's solutions only seem obvious in hindsight
Palm's WebOS had to independently solve the same problems since Apple hadn't yet shown its implementation, and Gruber finds Palm's version inferior
Apple's willingness to ship nothing rather than something mediocre is a core company philosophy that frustrates people who'd prefer a poor implementation over none at all
The two-year wait for copy and paste is evidence that 'good is the enemy of great' — a principle most tech companies reject
money_quotes: It all seems fairly obvious once you've used it, but there was nothing obvious about it before it was designed.
That we had to wait two years for the iPhone's text selection and pasteboard is a good example of one aspect of the Apple way: better nothing at all than something less than great.

It's simply incomprehensible to some people that it might be better to have no text selection/pasteboard implementation while waiting for a great one than to have a poor implementation in the interim.
It's hard to prove that good is the enemy of great, but the evidence speaks for itself.
This is contrary to the philosophy of most other tech companies — and diametrically opposed to the philosophy of Microsoft.
The selection and pasteboard features were simply too good, too useful, to go back to using an iPhone without them.

Without copy-and-paste, the only accurate way to quote even just a few sentences from one web page and insert them in the DF entry would have been to transcribe the passage by hand with paper and pen, then re-type the passage on the iPhone.
key_insight: Apple's two-year delay on iPhone copy and paste wasn't a failure but a deliberate refusal to ship a mediocre solution, and the elegant result vindicates the philosophy that it's better to have nothing than something less than great.
